cap_space_post_fa: TBD — Bills remain over cap pre-league-year; further restructures of Josh Allen contract and others expected before March 11 compliance deadline

key_departures.impact: 8-year Bill and cornerstone of the 'Buffalo Nickel' slot corner role. Initially released, then traded to Las Vegas for a 2026 sixth-round pick (Bills sent back a seventh). His play had declined from his 2023 All-Pro season and his skill set did not fit new DC Jim Leonhard's 3-4 scheme. Directly creates a slot CB need, which the Bills immediately addressed by signing Dee Alford.

Starting left guard departed for a massive 4-year, $61M deal with the Saints — a contract worth upwards of $15M/year that Buffalo was unwilling or unable to match given cap constraints. Bills retain center Connor McGovern but the LG spot is now vacant. Alec Anderson (1yr/$2.5M) is the in-house fallback, but he is depth/backup caliber, not a starter-quality replacement.

Released as a cap casualty, saving ~$6M. Samuel played only 6 games in 2025 due to injuries and never found consistent footing in Buffalo. Departure is a net neutral given the simultaneous trade for DJ Moore; WR room is effectively upgraded despite the exit.

Released as a cap casualty (failed physical per reports), saving ~$3.1M. Rapp was limited to 6 games in 2025 due to a knee injury. His departure leaves Cole Bishop as the lone reliable starter at safety. The S2 spot opposite Bishop is now a significant need.
Released as a cap casualty, saving ~$1.2M. Primarily a backup and special-teamer in recent seasons. Depth/special teams loss only.
Josh Allen's trusted backup departed on a 2-year deal with Tennessee. Bills replaced him same day with Kyle Allen, so this is a straight swap at the QB2 role.

The starting fullback and a key cog in Buffalo's league-leading 2025 rushing attack signed with a division rival. Gilliam was an underrated contributor to the run game. Creates a FB need but Buffalo rarely drafts the position; likely addressed via late-round draft or camp signing.

As of March 10, Bosa is unsigned. Led the NFL with 5 forced fumbles in 2025 and was projected at ~$13.7M AAV. His void year already triggered $7.2M in dead cap for Buffalo. With cap constraints, re-signing appears unlikely but not impossible. If he leaves, EDGE is a significant need alongside Greg Rousseau.

As of March 10, Milano is unsigned. His void year triggered $11M in dead cap — the largest such charge among Bills UFAs. Projected market value only ~$4.6M AAV but his availability history (multiple injury-shortened seasons) complicates a return. A new DC in Leonhard favoring a 3-4 scheme also changes the LB fit. If gone, an already thin LB room becomes a priority need.
As of March 10, Epenesa is unsigned. Was a quality rotational EDGE behind Bosa and Rousseau. His void year triggered $2.8M in dead cap. Losing him deepens the EDGE depth concern if Bosa also departs.

As of March 10, Poyer is unsigned. Veteran safety played well in 2025 as a starter opposite Cole Bishop. At 35 and with a projected market value of only ~$1.3M AAV, it is unclear if he returns. His departure (or non-return) further escalates the S need.
Veteran WR is a UFA. Given his age and minimal 2025 role, departure is expected. Minimal impact given the DJ Moore acquisition.

signings.trade_details: Bills sent 2026 2nd-round pick (No. 60 overall) to Chicago; received Moore and a 2026 5th-round pick

contract.notes: Moore's 2026 salary ($23.5M) fully guaranteed; 2027 base ($23.5M) guarantees on March 13; Bills also guaranteed $15.5M of 2028 salary as part of trade. Contract runs through 2029 at $24.5M/yr cash.

signings.analysis: A major acquisition that directly solves the Bills' WR1 problem. Moore takes over as Josh Allen's de facto No. 1 receiver and reunites with new HC Joe Brady, under whom he posted back-to-back 1,000+ yard seasons in Carolina. The contract is significant: 2026 and 2027 base salaries are both essentially fully guaranteed, and the Bills added guarantees to 2028. This is a committed investment, not a bridge. Bills gave up their 2026 2nd-round pick (No. 60) — meaningful draft capital cost. WR drops from a top need to a depth/developmental need. Khalil Shakir in the slot + Moore on the outside is a quality starter duo. Keon Coleman and Joshua Palmer remain in the mix for depth.

A strong re-signing that locks up the starting center at $13M AAV — the 3rd-highest paid center in the NFL per OTC, behind only Creed Humphrey and Cam Jurgens. McGovern graded as PFF's 9th-best center in 2025 and was fifth in pass-block win rate. The 4-year deal with $32M guaranteed signals a long-term commitment. However, left guard David Edwards simultaneously departed for the Saints (4yr/$61M), leaving a legitimate hole at LG. The IOL re-sign at center is fully resolved but LG remains an open question with only Alec Anderson (1yr/$2.5M) in-house. IOL need is partially addressed — center solved, guard is still a genuine need.

contract.notes: Reported max value up to $21M with incentives; base contract $15.75M per Aaron Wilson; $10.12M guaranteed; $4.5M signing bonus

signings.analysis: Alford is the first free agent signing under new DC Jim Leonhard and directly fills the Taron Johnson nickel vacancy. He's primarily a slot cornerback — the exact role Johnson vacated — and had a breakout 2025 season with Atlanta: 3 INTs, 13 PDs, 2 sacks in 64 games. The 3-year structure shows a real commitment, though the $5.25M AAV is below the CB starter tier. His guarantee ($10.12M over 3 years) is modest but workable for a nickel specialist. He partially addresses the CB need: the nickel slot is filled, but the Bills still lack CB depth behind Benford and Maxwell Hairston, and have no veteran outside CB signed. CB remains a real need at the outside spot and depth level.

contract.notes: Max value up to $6.1M with incentives per Schefter/Russini; Spotrac shows $300K signing bonus

signings.analysis: Direct replacement for Trubisky at QB2. Allen is a friend of Josh Allen, famously a qualification for backup QB in Buffalo. The 2-year deal is cheap insurance. Josh Allen hasn't missed a start since his rookie year, so this is low-stakes roster management. QB backup need is fully resolved — Shane Buechele provides QB3 insurance. No draft investment needed at QB.

contract.notes: Base contract $6.49M per Buffalo Rumblings/OTC; max $7.5M; $1.25M signing bonus; $2.53M fully guaranteed at signing (signing bonus + 2026 base salary)

signings.analysis: Franklin is a core special teams player who logged only 6 defensive snaps in 2025. The re-signing is about special teams retention, not plugging the starting safety hole created by Rapp's release and Poyer's uncertain status. At $2.5M AAV with only $2.53M guaranteed at signing, this is a depth/ST deal, not a starter contract. S remains a real need — Cole Bishop needs a starter-quality partner and Franklin is not that player.

contract.notes: Was a restricted free agent; re-signed to 1-year deal

signings.analysis: Anderson is the in-house option to compete for the vacant LG spot left by David Edwards. He has been a versatile backup who participated in all 34 games over two seasons in Buffalo. At 1yr/$2.5M with no guaranteed money, this is a depth re-sign, not a starter investment. With Edwards gone, Anderson is likely to start at LG by default unless Buffalo adds a free agent or drafts a guard. IOL LG remains a genuine need.

Mathis played 6 games with the Bills in 2025 after joining the team. He's a former 2nd-round pick with upside in Leonhard's 3-4. At $1.195M, this is a prove-it deal for a rotational IDL body. With DaQuan Jones, Jordan Phillips, and Larry Ogunjobi all unsigned UFAs, and Ed Oliver's contract restructured to keep him around, the IDL room needs more investment. This signing is low-impact depth retention.

contract.notes: Terms not disclosed; reported as a 1-year deal per Buffalo Rumblings

signings.analysis: Punter re-signed on a 1-year deal. No positional need affected.
Reserve/future deal to retain QB3. Standard depth move with Josh Allen as the unquestioned starter and Kyle Allen signed as QB2.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: The DJ Moore trade (BUF sending 2026 2nd-round pick to CHI) installs a legitimate WR1 in Josh Allen's arsenal. Moore's 2026 and 2027 base salaries are fully guaranteed ($23.5M each), making this a committed multi-year investment — not a bridge. Moore + Shakir in the slot is a quality starter duo. Keon Coleman and Joshua Palmer remain as depth/developmental pieces. WR drops from a critical need to a value pick in the mid-to-late rounds. They cost draft capital here, so drafting a WR early feels redundant unless a truly elite prospect falls.

Split verdict: center is locked up on a 4yr/$52M deal for Connor McGovern (3rd-highest paid center in NFL), fully resolving that position. However, starting LG David Edwards departed for 4yr/$61M with the Saints, and Alec Anderson (1yr/$2.5M, no guarantees) is the only in-house replacement. Anderson has been backup-caliber — the LG spot is a genuine need. IOL tier stays at 2 because the LG hole is real and opposing defenses will attack it. Priority guard target in rounds 2-4 of the draft or a veteran signing.

Dee Alford (3yr/$15.75M, $10.12M gtd) fills the nickel/slot role vacated by Taron Johnson — but at a depth AAV ($5.25M). The outside CB picture is thin: Christian Benford and Maxwell Hairston are the starters, with Alford in the slot. Tre'Davious White, Cam Lewis, and Darius Slay are all unsigned UFAs. The Bills need an outside CB with starter-level talent and durability. CB remains a tier 2 need, particularly for depth behind Benford and at CB2 in case of injury.

Taylor Rapp (starter) was released after failing a physical. Jordan Poyer (35, projected $1.3M AAV) is unsigned and uncertain. Cole Bishop is the only reliable starting safety currently under contract. Sam Franklin's re-sign is special-teams only (6 defensive snaps in 2025). The Bills are currently one injury away from having no experienced safety pairing. S has escalated from a moderate need to a top priority. Need to add a starting-caliber safety in FA or target in rounds 2-3 of the draft under new DC Leonhard's 3-4 system.

Both Joey Bosa (31, 5 sacks/5 FF in 2025, ~$13.7M AAV projected) and A.J. Epenesa (rotational, ~$6.4M AAV projected) are unsigned UFAs as of March 10. Their combined void-year dead cap ($7.2M for Bosa, $2.8M for Epenesa) already hit the books. Greg Rousseau is the lone signed EDGE starter. If Bosa departs, Buffalo's pass rush is Rousseau and depth. New DC Leonhard's 3-4 scheme relies on edge/OLB talent. EDGE escalates to a tier 1 need. Strong 2026 EDGE class makes this a prime draft target, and the Bills should also pursue a veteran free agent at the position.

Matt Milano (void year: $11M dead cap), Shaq Thompson, and Baylon Spector are all unsigned UFAs. Terrel Bernard (extended in 2025) is the one reliable LB under contract. Leonhard's 3-4 system is LB-heavy and requires coverage linebackers with range — a different profile than McDermott's 4-3. The LB room is extremely thin without Milano, who himself has a difficult injury history. LB escalates to a tier 1 priority. The Bills must address this in both free agency and the draft — rounds 2-4 should include at least one LB.

Ed Oliver (restructured, locked in) and Phidarian Mathis (1yr/$1.2M, rotational) are the known quantities. DaQuan Jones, Larry Ogunjobi, and Jordan Phillips are all unsigned UFAs. In Leonhard's 3-4, the nose tackle and IDL roles are critical for gap control. The Bills need at least one more proven IDL starter/rotational piece. IDL escalates from tier 3 to tier 2 — likely a mid-round draft pick or a mid-tier free agent signing needed.

Kyle Allen (2yr/$4.1M) replaces Trubisky 1-for-1 as the backup. Shane Buechele retained on reserve/future deal as QB3. Josh Allen is the unquestioned franchise QB. No draft investment warranted at QB — this is fully resolved.

key_additions.headline: Bills acquire their WR1 via trade, reuniting Moore with HC Joe Brady; Bills sent 2026 2nd-round pick to Chicago
key_additions.draft_cost: 2026 2nd-round pick (No. 60) sent to CHI; received 2026 5th-round pick in return

key_additions.headline: Pro Bowl center re-signed long-term at $13M AAV, locking up the most important OL piece ahead of free agency
Versatile slot CB signed to replace Taron Johnson at nickel in Leonhard's new 3-4 scheme
Josh Allen's former backup and close friend returns to Buffalo to fill the QB2 role vacated by Trubisky
Core special-teams ace re-signed on a 3-year deal; primarily a ST contributor, not a starter-level safety upgrade

key_losses.headline: Starting LG departs for massive payday to NFC South; leaves a genuine hole at guard with only Alec Anderson as in-house replacement

key_losses.contract_summary: Traded to LV Raiders (BUF received 6th-round pick, sent 7th)
key_losses.headline: 8-year franchise cornerstone and 2023 All-Pro nickel corner traded away; Dee Alford signed as direct replacement
Key fullback and lead blocker for Bills' No. 1 rushing offense departs to division rival New England
NFL's leader in forced fumbles (2025) is a cap casualty via void year; departure would leave Greg Rousseau as lone signed EDGE starter
Starting safety cut after failing physical; leaves Cole Bishop without a proven starting partner opposite him

key_losses.contract_summary: Unsigned UFA; $11M dead cap triggered via void year
key_losses.headline: Veteran starting LB unsigned entering free agency; his departure would gut an already thin linebacking corps under new 3-4 DC Leonhard
Injury-plagued WR cut after two disappointing seasons; departure offset by DJ Moore trade

notes: As of March 10 (legal tampering period; league year opens March 11), the following key UFAs remain unsigned and unresolved: Joey Bosa (EDGE), Matt Milano (LB), A.J. Epenesa (EDGE), Jordan Poyer (S), DaQuan Jones (IDL), Larry Ogunjobi (IDL), Jordan Phillips (IDL), Tre'Davious White (CB), Cam Lewis (CB), Shaq Thompson (LB). The Bills are over the salary cap (~-$12.9M per OTC) before further restructures; Josh Allen's contract restructure and other moves are expected before March 11 compliance deadline. The Taron Johnson situation was initially a release (announced March 6), then converted to a trade with the Raiders (announced March 8) — the trade is the operative transaction. DJ Moore's contract structure is unusual — he is carrying an existing Bears extension rather than signing a new deal; all values reflect the assumed contract terms as reported.
